patches.find: NOAA officially states no evidence of anomalous disappearances. Lloyd's of London does not charge premium rates for the region. U.S. Coast Guard reviews found no unusual causes. The dome's claim has two parts: (1) the regions are disappearance zones, and (2) the Bermuda and Japan regions share some form of 'Geometric Symmetry.' The dome publishes this as CONFIRMED with no tolerance specified. Under the quantitative reading (exact 180° antipodal symmetry), the regions miss by 28° (15.6%); under the vague reading ('notable' longitudinal symmetry without a specified tolerance), the claim is unfalsifiable. We evaluate the measurable, quantitative reading. Part (1) is debunked above. Part (2) conflates geographic folklore with geophysics — the Bermuda region is not a geomagnetic anomaly (the South Atlantic Anomaly, the actual major geomagnetic feature, is centered off the coast of Brazil, not Bermuda). The Japan region ("Devil's Sea") likewise has no distinctive geomagnetic signature. The dome's claimed 180° symmetry does not correspond to any pattern in IGRF or CHAOS-7 geomagnetic field models. Quantitatively: the Bermuda region (~25°N, 71°W) and Devil's Sea (~25°N, 137°E) are separated by 152° of longitude on the short arc (208° on the long arc) — 28° off the claimed 180°, a 15.6% deviation that exceeds the dome's own confirming thresholds — WIN-029 cites a 5.3% match as confirmation in another quantitative WIN. Neither agonic line passes through its associated region: the western agonic, currently near 95–100°W per IGRF/WMM 2025 (running through the central United States), lies well west of the Bermuda Triangle's eastern boundary at ~65°W, and the eastern agonic at ~100–120°E lies 17–37° west of the Devil's Sea (137°E).
patches.replace:
NOAA officially states no evidence of anomalous disappearances. Lloyd's of London does not charge premium rates for the region. U.S. Coast Guard reviews found no unusual causes. The dome's claim has two parts: (1) the regions are disappearance zones, and (2) the Bermuda and Japan regions share some form of "Geometric Symmetry."

The kernel. The Bermuda Triangle (~25°N, 71°W) and the Devil's Sea (~25°N, 137°E) really are near-opposite in longitude. Their longitudes differ by 152° going one way around the planet and 208° going the other; the two figures are conjugate (360° − 152° = 208°), so the deviation from a true 180° longitudinal symmetry is identical regardless of which direction one measures: |152 − 180| = |208 − 180| = 28°. (The great-circle distance between the two points is about 123°, but that is not the relevant quantity — "geometric symmetry" between two surface points at the same latitude maps onto longitudinal symmetry in both globe and flat-disc projections, and that is the measure the dome's claim invokes.) The two folklore zones do sit at roughly opposite hemispheres in this loose sense, and that is a real geographic observation.

Why the kernel does not save the claim. The dome's published title is "Bermuda Triangle / East Japan Geometric Symmetry CONFIRMED" with no specified tolerance. We do not attribute the literal number 180° to the dome; we use it because it is the only standard mathematical referent for "geometric symmetry" between two surface points (antipodal symmetry). With no published tolerance, the claim collapses into two readings, both of which fail under the dome's own confirmation standards:

• Quantitative reading (180° antipodal symmetry). The 28° gap is a 15.6% deviation. By the dome's own confirmation thresholds elsewhere in V51.1, that is a wide miss. WIN-029 takes a 5.3% agreement between the dome's quarter-wave Schumann calculation and its model height as confirmatory ("H = c/(4×7.83) = 9,572 km, 5.3% from model"). WIN-027 reports R² = 0.7874 for a fitted polynomial as confirmation. WIN-030 cites a 48.6% RMS reduction as a positive confirmation. A 15.6% off-target is roughly three times the residual the dome treats as a CONFIRMED match in WIN-029, and on the same order as the ratio errors the dome itself flagged as failures in earlier model versions ("v22 found circular disc fails on southern/cross-equator distances"). It is not a confirmatory match in the dome's own framework.

• Vague reading ("notable longitudinal coincidence" with no specified tolerance). Without a published threshold, any pair of points within roughly 50° of antipodal could pass, and the dome publishes no rule for which pairs do not. The claim becomes unfalsifiable: there is no observation that would convert the WIN to a refutation in the dome's ledger. An unfalsifiable claim cannot count as a CONFIRMED prediction in any framework that distinguishes confirmation from compatibility — including the dome's own, which lists "falsified" as a possible state.

The mechanism is also wrong. The dome's confirming text invokes the agonic lines (longitudes where magnetic declination = 0). Per IGRF-13 / WMM 2025, the western agonic currently runs near 95–100°W (through the central United States), 25–30° west of the Bermuda Triangle's eastern boundary at ~65°W. The eastern agonic at ~100–120°E lies 17–37° west of the Devil's Sea (137°E). Neither agonic line passes through its associated region. This is independent of the symmetry-tolerance debate: even granting "near-antipodal" loosely, the supposed mechanism (agonic-line correlation) does not hold quantitatively in either direction. The dome's claimed pattern does not correspond to any feature in IGRF or CHAOS-7 geomagnetic field models.

The disappearance claim itself is independently refuted. NOAA states there is no evidence of anomalous loss rates. Lloyd's of London does not levy higher premiums. The U.S. Coast Guard's 1975 review found no unusual causes. Even a perfectly antipodal pair of mid-latitude longitudes that happened to align with the agonic lines would not become "disappearance zones" without a physical loss-rate signal — and that signal is absent for both regions. The South Atlantic Anomaly, the actual major modern geomagnetic anomaly, is centered off Brazil, not Bermuda; the Devil's Sea likewise has no distinctive geomagnetic signature.
patches.rationale: EXP-298 defense neutralization: adds kernel-of-truth acknowledgment, explicit arc-direction calculation, anchors 15.6% to dome own thresholds (WIN-029 5.3%, WIN-027 R^2=0.7874, WIN-030 48.6%), adds unfalsifiability argument, pre-empts 4 main defender pivots.

patches.replace: 1.48% at the equator. The dome model has not specified its own aetheric falloff law, so we apply the Newtonian gravitational gradient (1/r³) as a baseline; the burden is on the dome to specify a propagation law that produces O1's observed latitude-dependent amplitude without this falloff.
patches.rationale: WIN-050.c4 moderate hole: pre-empt implicit Newtonian falloff assumption by stating it explicitly and placing burden of proof on dome.

patches.replace: the moon's declination (cycling between ±18.3° at minor standstill and ±28.6° at major standstill over the 18.6-year nodal cycle)
patches.rationale: WIN-050.c4 minor hole: 28.6 degrees is the major standstill maximum, not the typical declination. Express as the full nodal cycle range.

patches.find: (389 in the full Doodson expansion; 62 is a commonly cited practical subset)
patches.replace: (∼390 in the full Doodson expansion per Doodson 1921; 62 is a commonly cited practical subset)
patches.rationale: WIN-050.c4 minor hole: Doodson count 389 is uncertain across sources (388-396 cited variously). Hedge to ~390 and add citation.

patches.find: O1 (25.82 hours) depends on the moon's orbital motion relative to the rotating Earth.
patches.replace: O1 (25.82 hours) is the principal lunar diurnal constituent — it arises from the moon's declination cycle interacting with Earth's rotation, not merely from the lunar orbital period.
patches.rationale: WIN-050.c4 minor hole: opening sentence too generic. Dome defender can claim any orbiting moon satisfies it. Specify the declination-cycle mechanism.
